Highlights
----------

*   •Digital Twins have moved from idea to reality much faster in recent years. 
*   •The technology is poised to deliver upon its many promises in many industries. 
*   •The benefits of creating a Digital Twin solution are too vast and still not fully explored. 
*   •Digital Twins will combine with more technologies, such as augmented reality (AR) and AI for better connections, insights, and analytics. 

Abstract
--------

Applications of [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have been growing at an exponential rate, and it is transforming the way businesses operate. In the past few years, [Digital Twins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") leveraged vital [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and it is predicted that the technology will expand to more applications, use cases, and industries. The purpose of this paper is to do a literature review and explore how Digital Twins streamline intelligent automation in different industries. This paper defines the concept, highlights the evolution and development of Digital Twins, reviews its key enabling technologies, examines its trends and challenges, and explores its applications in different industries.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Digital Twin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is attracting attention from practitioners and scholars alike. Today, the technology is used across many industries to provide accurate virtual representations of objects and simulations of operational processes. In 2019, a Gartner survey revealed that Digital Twins were entering mainstream use by organizations. It predicted that 75 percent of [Internet of Things](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/internet-of-things "Learn more about Internet of Things from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IoT) organizations also use [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or plan to use it by 2020 [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b1), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b2). Gartner also estimates that by 2027, over 40 percent of large companies worldwide will be using Digital Twin in their projects to increase revenue [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b3).

Moreover, Global Market Insight estimated that the Digital Twin market size estimated in 2022 at $8 billion is expected to grow at around 25 percent [Compound Annual Growth Rate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/compound-annual-growth-rate "Learn more about Compound Annual Growth Rate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CAGR) from 2023 to 2032 [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b4). Finally, according to another recent report by global technology research, the Digital Twin market is set to grow by nearly $32 billion from 2021 to 2026 [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b5). In addition, according to a 2022 report, nearly 60 percent of executives across a broad spectrum of industry plan to incorporate Digital Twins within their operations by 2028 [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b6).

Digital Twin is a cutting-edge technology that has revolutionized the industry by mirroring almost every facet of a product, process, or service. It has the potential to replicate everything in the physical world in the digital space and provide engineers with feedback from the virtual world [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b7). As a result, the technology enables companies to quickly detect and solve physical problems, design and build better products, and realize value and benefits faster than previously possible. Furthermore, the Digital Twin technology enables businesses to improve business processes and performance [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b8).

The research started by developing key concepts based on a narrative literature review in the Scopus database and Google Scholar and searched for papers that included Digital Twin or Digital Twins in the title. Articles were selected from Journal articles, research articles, conference publications, research reports, or scientific encyclopedias. We reviewed the documents to find answers to the following questions: What is a Digital Twin, what technologies are used, what are the current applications of Digital Twins in different industries, and what are the challenges and opportunities facing this growing technology? We selected research papers that defined the concept, reviewed its related technologies, and highlighted its applications and trends in different industries. Section[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec2) provides the main perspectives and definitions of Digital Twins in literature. Section[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec3) reviews four enabling technologies of the Digital Twins. Section[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec4) studies Digital Twin applications and use cases of different industries. Section[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec5) highlights the challenges and opportunities of this technology. Finally, Section[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec6) provides a summary and conclusions. Section 7 provides references.

2. Background and definition
----------------------------

In 2003 Professor Grieves of the University of Michigan introduced the concept of [Digital Twins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in a total [product lifecycle management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/product-lifecycle-management "Learn more about product lifecycle management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") course. It is also known as a digital mirror and digital mapping. Since then, its definition has continued to evolve as several scholars have provided varied definitions of this technology [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b9), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b11), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b14). Encyclopedia of Production Engineering states that “_The Digital Twin is a representation of an active unique “product” which can be a real device, object, machine, service, intangible asset, or a system consisting of a product and its related services”_[[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12)_._ In general, the Digital Twin is defined as virtual representations of [physical objects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/physical-object "Learn more about physical objects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") across the lifecycle that can be understood, learned, and reasoned with real-time data or a simulation model that acquires data from the field and triggers the operation of physical devices [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b15), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b16).

Furthermore, Digital Twin was defined as the convergence between physical and virtual products [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b18). Fu et al. [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b7) thought of the Digital Twin as a real-time digital representation of a physical object. They are remotely connected to real objects and provide rich representations of these objects. They go beyond static product designs, like CAD models, but comprise dynamic behavior [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b19), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b20). A virtual replica of a real-world asset is obtained through constant data transmission allowing the digital version of the object to exist simultaneously with the physical one [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b17). Digital Twin uses [big data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/big-data "Learn more about big data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technology to mine hidden and effective data and to improve the intelligence and applicability of [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), especially for quick identification and evaluation of design flaws [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b21).

Finally, Kritzinger et al. [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13) defined Digital Twin, within the field of manufacturing, based on the data integration level, which can be achieved between the physical product and its virtual representation. He identified three levels of integration, The Digital Model, Digital Shadow and the Digital Twin ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig1)) [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b22).

A historic early application of Digital Twin technology is when NASA engineers used a simulator, a twin of the command module, and a separate twin of the module’s [electrical system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrical-system "Learn more about electrical system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to remedy and save Apollo 13 in 1970. NASA engineers completed the process in under two hours and saved the lives of the three astronauts on board. This was an extraordinary early application of this technology, and the technology has only matured since then [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b23). Today, NASA uses Digital Twins to develop next-generation vehicles and aircraft.

The concept of Digital Twins is not new. However, Digital Twins have moved from idea to reality much faster in recent years. It is predicted that Digital Twins will be combined with more technologies such as speech capabilities, [augmented reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/augmented-reality "Learn more about augmented reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [IoT](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/internet-of-things "Learn more about IoT from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [artificial intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence "Learn more about artificial intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AI). As a result, Gartner included Digital Twins on its list of top 10 technology trends for 2017 [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b24). Gartner also predicted that half of the large industrial firms to use Digital Twins in crucial [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by 2021 [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b24). Finally, MarketsandMarkets research predicted rapid growth for the Digital Twin technology within the next few years, thanks to rising interest in the manufacturing industry to reduce cost and improve [supply chain operations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/supply-chain-operation "Learn more about supply chain operations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). As a result, the market for Digital Twin technology was valued at $6.9 billion in 2022. However, it is expected to reach $73.5 billion by 2027- a [CAGR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/compound-annual-growth-rate "Learn more about CAGR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of more than 60 percent [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b25).

3. Digital twin technologies
----------------------------

The three main aspects of Digital Twins are data acquisition, [data modeling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/data-modeling "Learn more about data modeling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and data application [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b26). Digital Twin uses four technologies to collect and store real-time data, obtain information to provide valuable insights, and create a digital representation of a [physical object](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/physical-object "Learn more about physical object from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). These technologies include the Internet of Things (IoT), [Artificial Intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence "Learn more about Artificial Intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AI), [Extended Reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/extended-reality "Learn more about Extended Reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (XR), and Cloud ([Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig2)). In addition, Digital Twin uses a particular technology, depending on the application type, to a greater or lesser extent.

*   1._Internet of Things (IoT):_ IoT refers to a giant network of connected “things”. The connection is between things–things, people–things, or people–people [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b27). Digital Twins use IoT as its primary technology in every application. By 2027, more than 90 percent of all IoT platforms will have Digital Twinning capability [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b6). IoT uses sensors to collect data from real-world objects. The data transmitted by IoT is used to create a digital duplication of a physical object. The digital version then can then be analyzed, manipulated, and optimized. IoT constantly updates data and helps Digital Twin applications create a real-time virtual representation of a physical object. Therefore, every Digital Twin application uses IoT as a primary technology. 
*   2._[Cloud Computing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/cloud-computing "Learn more about Cloud Computing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_: Cloud computing refers to delivering hosted services over the Internet. The technology efficiently stores and accesses data over the Internet [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b27). Cloud computing provides Digital Twins with data computing technology and cloud data storage technology. Cloud computing allows Digital Twin, with large volumes of data, to store data in the virtual Cloud and easily access the required information from any location. Cloud computing enables Digital Twins to effectively reduce the [computation time](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/computation-time "Learn more about computation time from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of complex systems and overcome the difficulties of storing large amounts of data [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b28). 
*   3.[Artificial Intelligence (AI)](https://softengi.com/expertise/artificial-intelligence/): As a discipline of computer science, AI seeks to mimic the basis of intelligence to create a new intelligent machine capable of responding like human-to-human intelligence. Areas of AI study include Robotics, image recognition, and language recognition. [Neural Networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/neural-network "Learn more about Neural Networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Machine Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/machine-learning "Learn more about Machine Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Deep Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/deep-learning "Learn more about Deep Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [expert systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/expert-system "Learn more about expert systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b29), AI can assist Digital Twins by providing an advanced analytical tool capable of automatically analyzing obtained data and providing valuable insights, making predictions about outcomes, and giving suggestions as to how to avoid potential problems [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b26). 
*   4.[Extended Reality (XR)](https://softengi.com/expertise/expanded-reality/) is an umbrella term used to describe immersive technologies like Virtual Reality (VR), [Augmented Reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/augmented-reality "Learn more about Augmented Reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AR), and Mixed Reality (MR). These technologies can merge the physical and virtual worlds and extend the reality we experience [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b30). XR creates digital representations of objects where digital and real-world objects co-exist and interact in real-time. Digital Twins utilize XR capabilities to digitally model physical objects, allowing users to interact with digital content.

4. Digital twins use cases and applications
-------------------------------------------

Today, engineering and manufacturing predominantly use Digital Twins to provide accurate virtual representations of objects and simulations of operational processes. Digital Twins applications in operations and supply chain management, especially the role of Digital Twins in terms of operations traceability, transport maintenance, remote assistance, asset visualization, and design [customization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/customisation "Learn more about customization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), are reviewed in related publications [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b31), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b32), [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b33), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b34), [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b35), [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b36), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b37), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b38). What is Digital Twinning in Construction?
-----------------------------------------

Digital twinning in construction is a game-changer. It involves creating a digital replica of a physical asset. This technology is revolutionizing how we build and manage structures. Understanding its roots and applications can help you see its importance.

### Definition of Digital Twins and Their Origins

A [digital twin](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/)A digital twin is a virtual replica or simulation of a physical object, process, or system. It is a digital model that r… [More](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/) is a digital copy of a real-world object. This object could be anything from a building to a car. The concept of digital twins isn’t new. NASA first used it during the Apollo 13 mission in 1970. When the mission faced problems, NASA used a [digital twin](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/)A digital twin is a virtual replica or simulation of a physical object, process, or system. It is a digital model that r… [More](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/) to solve the issues from thousands of miles away.

### Explanation of Digital Twins in Construction

In construction, digital twins mean creating an exact digital version of a project. This could be a building, bridge, or even an entire city. With digital twins, you can monitor and manage every aspect of the construction process. For example, using 3D models and real-time data from sensors, a [digital twin](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/)A digital twin is a virtual replica or simulation of a physical object, process, or system. It is a digital model that r… [More](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/) can show you the current state of a building and predict future problems.

### Examples of Digital Twins in Other Industries

Digital twins are used in many industries:

*   **Automotive**: Car manufacturers use digital twins to test and improve vehicle designs.
*   **Healthcare**: Hospitals use digital twins to simulate surgeries and improve patient care.
*   **Aerospace**: Companies like Boeing use digital twins to monitor airplane performance and maintenance needs.

### Importance of Digital Twins in Modern Construction Projects

Digital twins are vital in modern construction for several reasons:

1.   **Accuracy**: They provide a precise digital model that reflects real-world conditions.
2.   **Efficiency**: By simulating changes in the [digital twin](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/)A digital twin is a virtual replica or simulation of a physical object, process, or system. It is a digital model that r… [More](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/), you can avoid costly mistakes on the actual site.
3.   **Management**: Digital twins help in managing resources and predicting maintenance needs.
4.   **Collaboration**: They allow different teams to work together more effectively by sharing a single source of truth.

Understanding digital twinning in construction can transform how you approach projects. It gives you the tools to create more accurate, efficient, and coordinated building processes.

How to Create Digital Twins for Construction Projects
-----------------------------------------------------

Creating digital twins for construction projects involves several key technologies and processes. Understanding these can help you build accurate and effective digital models.

### Technologies Used in Creating Digital Twins

**3D Laser Scanners**: These devices capture detailed images of construction sites. They produce high-resolution, 3D models that serve as the foundation for digital twins. Using laser scanners ensures that every detail is captured accurately.

**Drones**: Drones are used to take aerial photos and videos of construction sites. They can reach places that are difficult or dangerous for humans. The data collected by drones is used to create up-to-date, 3D maps of the site.

**Sensors**: Sensors are placed throughout a construction site to monitor various factors like temperature, humidity, and structural integrity. These sensors provide real-time data that keeps the [digital twin](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/)A digital twin is a virtual replica or simulation of a physical object, process, or system. It is a digital model that r… [More](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/) current.

### Data Integration from Various Sources

Digital twins rely on data from multiple sources to be accurate and useful. Two key technologies make this possible:

**Internet of Things (IoT)**: IoT devices collect and send data from the physical world to the [digital twin](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/)A digital twin is a virtual replica or simulation of a physical object, process, or system. It is a digital model that r… [More](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/). Examples include smart sensors and connected equipment. This data allows the [digital twin](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/)A digital twin is a virtual replica or simulation of a physical object, process, or system. It is a digital model that r… [More](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/) to reflect real-time conditions.

**Artificial Intelligence (AI)**: AI analyzes the data collected by IoT devices. It can identify patterns and make predictions. For instance, AI can predict when a piece of equipment might fail, allowing for preventive maintenance.

### Steps to Ensure Accurate and Current Digital Twins

1.   **Initial Scanning**: Begin by using 3D laser scanners to create a detailed model of the site.
2.   **Data Collection**: Use drones and sensors to collect additional data. This data should cover all aspects of the construction process.
3.   **Integration:** Combine data from different sources. Use IoT devices to ensure real-time updates.
4.   **AI Analysis**: Apply AI to analyze the data. This step helps in identifying patterns and making predictions.
5.   **Regular Updates**: Continuously update the [digital twin](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/)A digital twin is a virtual replica or simulation of a physical object, process, or system. It is a digital model that r… [More](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/digital-twin/) with new data. This ensures that the model remains accurate and current.

**Formula 1 Racing**: The automotive industry uses digital twins to improve car performance. Teams use sensors to collect data during races. This data is analyzed in real-time to make adjustments and improve performance.

**Healthcare**: Hospitals use digital twins to simulate surgeries. Real-time data from medical sensors helps create accurate models of patients. These models allow doctors to plan and practice surgeries, improving outcomes.

Building digital twins involves advanced technologies and thorough processes. By combining 3D [laser scanning](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/laser-scanning/)Laser scanning is a technology used to capture and create highly-detailed and accurate digital representations of physic… [More](https://www.datumate.com/glossary/laser-scanning/), drones, sensors, IoT, and AI, you can create accurate, current digital models. These models are invaluable for managing and optimizing construction projects.

How to Use Digital Twins in Construction Projects
-------------------------------------------------

Digital twins offer numerous advantages to construction projects. They enhance the analytical capabilities of Building Information Modeling (BIM), allow for digital experimentation, support ongoing structural enhancements, and streamline facility management.

### Improving Analytical Capabilities of BIM

**Building Information Modeling (BIM)**already provides a comprehensive way to manage construction projects. However, integrating digital twins takes BIM to the next level. Digital twins offer real-time data, making BIM models more dynamic and accurate.

*   **Enhanced Visualization:** Digital twins provide a more detailed and real-time view. This helps architects and engineers see how changes in one part of the project affect the rest of the structure.
*   **Real-Time Updates:** Digital twins continuously update with live data from sensors and IoT devices unlike traditional BIM models. This feature ensures that you always have the most current information.

### Experimenting Digitally Before Making Physical Changes

One of the most significant benefits of digital twins is the ability to experiment digitally before implementing physical changes. This capability can save time and resources.

*   **Simulation**: You can simulate various construction scenarios to see potential outcomes. For example, test different materials or structural designs to identify the most efficient options.
*   **Risk Mitigation**: By experimenting digitally, you can identify and mitigate risks before they become real-world problems. This helps in avoiding costly mistakes and delays.

### Supporting Ongoing Structural Enhancements

Digital twins don’t just help during the initial construction phase; they are invaluable for ongoing structural enhancements.

*   **Maintenance Planning**: Use the data from digital twins to plan maintenance activities. Predictive analytics can identify when parts of the building will need repairs, allowing for proactive maintenance.
*   **Upgrades and Modifications**: When upgrading or modifying a structure, digital twins provide a detailed, real-time view. This helps in planning and executing changes more effectively.

### Streamlining Facility Management and Improving Efficiencies

After construction is complete, digital twins continue to offer benefits in facility management. They help in streamlining operations and improving efficiencies.

*   **Centralized Data**: All data related to the building is stored in one place. Facility managers can easily access information about the building’s systems, materials, and condition.

**Automated Monitoring**: Sensors and IoT devices continuously monitor the building’s performance. This data can be used to optimize energy use, manage space more efficiently, and improve the overall operation of the facility.

# Digital twins and Infrastructure Management, Operations, and Exploitation

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHdpZHRoPSIyNTYwIiBoZWlnaHQ9IjI1NjAiIHZpZXdCb3g9IjAgMCAyNTYwIDI1NjAiPjxyZWN0IHdpZHRoPSIxMDAlIiBoZWlnaHQ9IjEwMCUiIHN0eWxlPSJmaWxsOiNjZmQ0ZGI7ZmlsbC1vcGFjaXR5OiAwLjE7Ii8+PC9zdmc+)
![](https://streamlinevn.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/digital_twins-scaled.jpg)

The development of infrastructure plays a key role in economic growth. Therefore, effective management and operation of technical infrastructure of projects (such as buildings, commercial centers, urban areas, data centers, industrial parks,…) is an important factor to increase asset value for investors and owners. However, infrastructure management and operations units are facing many challenges. This article will analyze those challenges, and introduce digital twins technology on a 360-degree photo platform and its applications to solve these challenges.

## **Challenges in operating, managing and exploiting infrastructure**

The technical infrastructure of projects is very diverse, depending on the type and scale of the project. This poses many challenges for management units in effective operation, exploitation and maintenance, especially when there is no optimal management method.

### Scale and diversity of infrastructure

Urban and industrial park infrastructure includes a variety of fields such as transportation, energy, lighting, drainage, telecommunications… Office building infrastructure for rent includes many systems such as electricity, water, air conditioning, fire protection, CCTV… Each type of infrastructure includes many technical components or subsystems with separate technological requirements and operating documents. This makes it difficult to manage and access documents during operation, especially when the infrastructure scale is large and widely distributed.

### Complex dependencies and interactions between infrastructures

Infrastructures are always closely related and influence each other. A failure in one infrastructure can cause a chain reaction, affecting entire systems, such as electrical and air conditioning systems. The lack of a holistic perspective and synchronized coordination between stakeholders in operations management can lead to delays in determining the cause and fixing problems, affecting customer experience.

### Huge and diverse amount of data

Big data from sensors and monitoring devices is very diverse, including data, images, videos, maps, 3D models… This data is located on many different systems. Because these systems do not interact with each other, synthesizing and recording into a common data warehouse takes a lot of time, leading to a large volume of data, making it difficult to transmit, manage and centrally exploit.

### The developments are fast and unpredictable

Incidents in daily operations and infrastructure operations are very diverse and can originate from human or technical factors. They all happen quickly, frequently and are difficult to predict. If not detected, handled and notified promptly, incidents can cause confusion for customers and affect service quality.

### Resources are limited and uneven

Human and financial resources for infrastructure management and operation of each project are often limited. Therefore, optimizing and saving operating management costs is necessary, but still ensuring service quality. This puts pressure on the management department to improve efficiency and competitiveness.

### Information safety and security

Infrastructure is increasingly digitized. This increases the risk of security vulnerabilities. If not detected and fixed promptly, these vulnerabilities can create opportunities for cyber attacks, causing system paralysis and affecting user experience.

To effectively solve the above challenges, applying digital technology is inevitable. In particular, applying digital twin technology on a 360-degree image platform to the management, operation and exploitation of infrastructure will help units centrally manage and monitor the entire project remotely, thereby saving travel time costs for evaluation and inspection.

## Introducing digital twins on a 360o photo platform

A Digital Twin is an exact digital model of a physical object or system. It acts as a comprehensive platform that spans the building lifecycle, enabling the integration of IoT data with location information, device management, documentation, processes and interactions. Thanks to that, all activities from construction implementation, inspection and supervision to maintenance and training are digitized, comprehensively changing the supply chain and operations in infrastructure management and exploitation.

## Applying 360o solutions digital twin

Digital twin technology based on 360o photo platformthe has been widely applied in the management of technical infrastructure, helping to solve challenges in operating and exploiting infrastructure, typically:

### Project management

### Build project documents

### Inspection and supervision work

Abstract
--------

When combined with information and communication technologies and powerful data analytic algorithms such as artificial intelligence, digital twins enable organisations to conserve physical resources. This applies both during the design phase and when performing diagnostic and predictive analyses during operations. These abilities bring significant opportunities to the infrastructure industry to develop new ways of designing, constructing, operating and monitoring infrastructure at a time when much of the world’s civil infrastructure is ageing and showing signs of deterioration. This study aims to find out how digital twins can help the infrastructure industry to deliver and operate sustainable and smart infrastructure assets. This paper presents an overview of digital twin definitions, current practices, benefits and challenges through a series of semi-structured expert interviews with executives from the UK infrastructure industry. Additionally, it suggests a series of strategies to aid digital transformation and digital twin adoption in the industry. Results from the interviews illustrated that the executives involved in digital transformation in the infrastructure industry are very well aware of the definitions, benefits and challenges of digital twins. In general, they understand the value of digital transformation and specifically digital twins. They know the reasons behind the need for transforming the industry and adopting data-driven concepts such as digital twins. Moreover, the executives interviewed as part of this study mentioned common challenges across different infrastructure domains. The strategies presented are focused on addressing these three main challenges identified and agreed upon by the participants – culture, technology adoption and lack of a skilled workforce. The three main strategies, addressing digital transformation (1), cultural transformation (2) and bridging the skills gap (3), are explained later in this paper. The article concludes by underlining the importance of creating equal opportunities for the current workforce to improve their digital fluency and skillset by providing information about the benefits of digital twins throughout the sector and organisations to improve adoption and the realisation of benefits.

Introduction
------------

Since it was introduced by Michael Grieves in 2003, the term “digital twin” (Grieves and Vickers [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)) has experienced a growth in popularity and is now recognised as a key enabler for transformation to Industry 4.0. In the last two decades, many digital twin applications have been presented regarding space (Glaessgen and Stargel [Citation 2012](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)) and manufacturing (Schleich et al. [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#); Qi and Tao [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#); Tao et al. [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)) industries. A digital twin in the built environment domain has been defined as a realistic digital representation of assets, processes or systems (Bolton et al. [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). It mirrors physical, social and/or economic systems and the processes that are articulated alongside the system in question, and across its lifecycle, mimicking its operation in real-time (Batty [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)).

There are different approaches to implementing digital twins. While some (Rudrappa [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)) provide classifications such as digital twin prototypes, digital twin instances, digital twin aggregates and digital twin environments, others divide the digital twins as status twin, operational twin or simulation twin (XMPRO, [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). Furthermore, digital twins can consist of several components such as 3 D models, Internet of Things (IoT), sensors, data models, artificial intelligence and machine learning-enabled analytics and algorithms, and knowledge (Rudrappa [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). When combined with information and communication technologies and powerful data analytic algorithms such as artificial intelligence, digital twins allow organisations to conserve physical resources during design and perform diagnostic and predictive analyses during operations (Grieves and Vickers [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)).

Much of the world’s civil infrastructure is ageing and showing significant levels of deterioration (Hoult et al. [Citation 2009](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#); Gürdür Broo and Schooling [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). Ageing assets are not only a financial burden for society but also affect the environment and the overall sustainability of the planet (Van Breugel [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). In addition, the delivery of major infrastructure projects has been slow and uncertain (National Infrastructure Commission, 2018). The delivery of infrastructure projects has major effects on sustainable development (Shen et al. [Citation 2011](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). There are some sustainability-focused approaches for building construction that supports life-cycle thinking across three sustainability dimensions – economic, environmental and societal. However, there are few integral approaches specifically designed for the sustainability assessment of infrastructures (Bocchini et al. [Citation 2014](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). This needs to change because infrastructure systems form the backbone of every society, providing essential services that include energy, water, waste management, transport and telecommunications (Thacker et al. [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). More attention should be brought to developing new ways of designing, constructing, operating, and monitoring infrastructure to better understand current and future needs and the relationship with sustainability.

Digital twins provide an opportunity to collect and integrate data for improving the design, construction, operation, and maintenance of physical infrastructure assets which, in turn, could improve sustainable development. This study approached executives leading digital transformation in the biggest infrastructure projects of the United Kingdom to answer one question:

RQ 1: How can digital twins help the infrastructure industry to deliver and operate sustainable and smart infrastructure assets?

 To answer this question, we have divided it into three sub-questions (SRQ):
*   SRQ 1: How does the definition of digital twins differ for different infrastructure projects?

*   SRQ 2: What are the main benefits of digital twins?

*   SRQ 3: What are the challenges of implementing and operationalising digital twins?

The main objective of this study is to provide an overview of the current digital twin practices of the United Kingdom’s infrastructure sector by summarizing the definitions, benefits, and challenges. While digital twin implementations, case studies and literature reviews from other domains exist; this particular focus on current practices and collecting experiences and learning from the practitioners in the infrastructure sector has not been discussed in the literature prior to this study. Moreover, the study aims to present series of strategies that can be useful for not only the studied region but in a wider context when infrastructure digital twins are developed by other countries and regions.

The original contribution of this paper consists of an industrial overview of digital twin definitions, current practices, benefits, and challenges through a series of semi-structured expert interviews with executives from the UK infrastructure industry. Additionally, it delivers a series of strategies to aid digital transformation and digital twin adoption by the industry.

To this end, Section “Related work” presents the earlier work on the research topic. Section “Methodology” details the research methodology. Later in Section “Findings”, the findings of the study are presented. Before concluding the article in Section “Conclusion”, we discuss the findings and strategies in Section “Discussion”.

Related work
------------

Several researchers have studied the potential of using big data in smart infrastructure, specifically in a smart grid or city context. Some studies focused on socio-economic problems and suggested using data to form collective awareness for creating innovative solutions (Pitt et al. [Citation 2013](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). Other studies looked at infrastructure from an efficiency perspective and included data resources from surveying, communication, geospatial and network applications to manage power consumption and operational resources (Al-Hader and Rodzi [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#); Le et al. [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#); Akterujjaman et al. [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). Recent literature is specifically rich when it comes to providing solutions to technical problems related to networking (Idwan et al. [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#); Ota et al. [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#); Das et al. [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)), monitoring (Alavi et al. [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#); Heaton et al. [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#); Chung et al. [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)), blockchain (Kiu et al. [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)) or in general emerging technologies (Li and Liu [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#); Noruwa et al. [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). However, very few papers focus on the multidisciplinary aspects (Ogie et al. [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)), digital transformation (Zomer et al., 2019) and data challenges related to these systems (Bowers et al. [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#); Gürdür Broo and Schooling [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). Several researchers looked at the construction and infrastructure industry from a management perspective considering smart decision making through data-driven approaches. While some focused on implications of industry 4.0 (García de Soto et al. [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)) or the effect of digitalisation (Aghimien et al. [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)), others focused more on the improving performance of the infrastructure industry (McDermot et al. [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)), proposed frameworks to assessing the sustainability of the infrastructures (Siew et al. [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)).

Early definitions of a digital twin, such as the one from Grieves and Vickers ([Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)), presented the concept with basic characteristics (Trauer et al. [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)) of a ‘real space’ and a ‘virtual space’ connected via data and information exchange (Grieves and Vickers [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). ‘Real’ is also later referred to as ‘physical’ and ‘virtual’ as ‘cyber’. This division of physical and cyber parts is also seen as an evolution from Gelernter's ([Citation 1991](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)) “mirror worlds” concept which can be expressed as, “software programs… about to be joined by vast public software works that will revolutionize computing and transform society as a whole”.

The most widely used definition of a digital twin is from NASA which states that the digital twin is an “integrated multi-physics, multi-scale, probabilistic simulation of a vehicle or system that uses the best available physical models, sensor updates, fleet history, etc., to mirror the life of its corresponding flying twin” (Shafto et al. [Citation 2012](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). Some definitions such as the one from Deloitte ([Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)), “a near-real-time digital image of a physical object or process that helps optimize business performance”, focus on the speed of updating the digital/cyber representation. A more current definition in manufacturing defines the term as “… a digital representation of an active unique product […] or unique product-service system […] that comprises its selected characteristics, properties, conditions, and behaviours by means of models, information, and data within a single or even across multiple life cycle phases” (Stark and Damerau [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)). Digital twins in infrastructure is a new concept and there is no commonly accepted definition of the term. The Centre for Digital Built Britain defines it as “a realistic digital representation of assets, processes or systems in the built or natural environment” (Bolton et al. [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)).

Barricelli et al. ([Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/15623599.2021.1966980#)) conducted an extensive survey on digital twin definitions, characteristics, applications and design implications where they have reviewed 75 papers from aviation, hospital management, manufacturing, precision medicine and manufacturing. enriched_points.expanded_content: Infrastructure often incurs significant maintenance costs due to aging assets, requiring advanced monitoring and management to optimize resources and reduce expenditures.
Poor visibility into infrastructure performance leads to suboptimal resource distribution, resulting in wasted materials, time, and labor across various projects.
Traditional systems lack real-time analytics, hindering proactive maintenance and emergency response, which can lead to unexpected failures and costly repairs.
Fragmented data storage across departments complicates access and analysis, making it difficult to leverage insights for informed decision-making in infrastructure management.
